replies.text: You and me both. This video he has made has moved me to tears. You get numb to what is destroyed, and what is lost. And the worst quality she has of all, the fact she thinks she is so wonderful and has no perception of how wrong she is. Five minutes in a room with her and I would want to smash something...And Gatwa manages to be be even worse. How anyone with more than 2 brain cells could watch his run as the Doctor is beyond me, the ratings were too high. Far too many still watched. All that money wasted on that utter mush, and two minutes seeing clips of the classic era showed up all the modern writing for the utter toss it is. Tom Baker, Pertwee, Troughton, Hartnell on and on, superb acting, magnificent voices, gravitas, charisma, charm and the utter and total believability to sell the story, scene after scene. This was an obituary for my show and any true Doctor Who fan. It hurts all over again, to see the death and destruction of a true friend.

author: @oo9-v7i
text: People often say "Well, Doctor Who always had a liberal slant." They're not exactly wrong, but back in the day it was a genuine, classical liberal, viewpoint. It was openminded and questioned authority. It's a bit like saying "Well, my friend always enjoyed a small sherry." while you're watching them writhing on the floor, choking on their own vomit, trousers soaked in pee, surrounded by empty bottles of absinth and drain cleaner.

author: @eugeneshadwell6596
text: 55 year old UK guy here, DW was the longest televisual relationship of my life.  Old enough to remember Jon Pertwee (although Tom Baker was 'my' Doctor as a kid), bought all the toys and Target novelizations, stayed with the show throughout the lean years and then weathered  the 16 year hiatus.  A grown man with a kid of my own when the show returned, we watched the show together and I saw her loving it just as much as I did way back when. And we've both fallen out of love with it over the last few years, a brilliant show wrecked by sheer hubris and blinkered thinking. I know, it's 'only' a TV show but I'm just so damn saddened by the way things turned out.  Great video, Gary, genuinely gave me a lump in the throat at the end.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: Exactly the same. The same age. Same experiences. The Tom Baker era Target novels as written by Terrance Dicks taught me to read. And I went from being bottom of the class in English, to top of the class in the junior school in about six months thanks to Doctor Who and the books.  I went on to take O level and then A level and study journalism and creative writing which I still practice to this day. Its a hobby as the computer bug made my career...once again thanks to K9, Doctor Who and Star Wars etc. The final section of this video had me weeping for what we have all lost as fans, and also cheering as it ended on Capaldi and it was a return of the middle finger to Davies and the BBC. The same as they gave us as fans...well this video gave them it back....

replies.author: @TF2CrunchyFrog
replies.text:
​@SomeDigitalGhost You _do_ know_ the Library of Alexandria in Egypt burned down many times, yes? It was a building full of wooden shelves and scrolls of parchment and papyrus, lit by oil maps. And it was rebuild, many times. It slowly started to dwindle in cultural importance during the late Roman era _due to budget cuts._ It final end came in 642 AD, when the city of Alexandria was captured by an Arab army under the command of Amr ibn al-As. Several later Arabic sources describe the library's destruction by the order of Caliph Umar.

But even after the most "famous" fire in 48 BC, when Julius Caesar was besieged at Alexandria [the culturally _Greek_ city of Alexandria, founded by the Macedonia-Greek general Alexander the Great], the Library continued to exist. Caesar's soldiers had set fire to some of the Egyptian ships docked in the Alexandrian port while trying to clear the wharves to block the fleet belonging to Cleopatra's brother Ptolemy XIV; This fire purportedly spread to the parts of the city nearest to the docks, destroying dockyards storehouses for grain and books, according to Roman historian Cassius Dio. But various contemporary Roman historians' accounts disagree on how much of the Library was destroyed. Titus Livius wrote that 40,000 scrolls were destroyed by the fire, indicating the whole or parts of the Library proper were affected. Other historians claimed only buildings at the docks were burned down, which might have included the Mouseion, the larger research institution to which the Library was attached. But the Roman geographer Strabo (c. 63 BC – c. 24 AD) mentions visiting the Mouseion in around 20 BC, several decades after Caesar's fire, indicating that it either survived the fire or was rebuilt soon afterwards.

All of this happened during the reign of Queen Cleopatra VII, who was Pharaoh of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt from 51 to 30 BC, during the Hellenistic period when the Greeks established city state colonies as far east as Anatolia. (The Turk tribes from the Central Asian steppes only invaded Anatolia much later, in the 11th century AD.) The Ptolemaic dynasty was founded by the Macedonian-Greek General Ptolomy I Soter, a general of Alexander's, who was appointed satrap of Egypt after Alexander's death in 323 BC. In 305 BC he declared himself Pharaoh Ptolemy I, preserving Egyptian cultural traditions while blending Egyptian and Greek cultures. Lasting for 275 years, the Ptolemaic was the longest and final dynasty of ancient Egypt from 305 BC until its incorporation into the Roman Republic in 30 BC, and is remembered by Egyptians as an era when Ancient Egypt flourished.

author: @Stumbo99
text:
My dad found this show around 2006 or 2007, and he then proceeded to show it to my brother and I. It became our bonding show, something that we desperately needed after our parents had gotten divorced and we were left emotionally scarred and confused. This was the show we'd sit down and watch together, us 3, and bond and get lost in the scifi fun and beautiful horror that was the Doctor. 
I've lost many things to the culture war, my love for comics, movie, video games, even anime and manga were and are tainted but Doctor Who is quite possibly the most important and heartbreaking to lose. It went from a show i loved with all my heart for what it gave to my family and I, to a show that brings me visceral disgust and mourning for what once was. 

RIP Doctor Who, and thank you for everything

author: @Eron_the_Relentless
text:
Last words of the Doctor:

Oh, there it is. Silly old universe. The more I save it the more it needs saving. It’s a treadmill.
Yes, yes I know they’ll get it all wrong without me.
Well, I suppose….one more lifetime won’t kill anyone. Well, except me.
You wait a moment, Doctor. Let’s get it right. I’ve got a few things to say to you. Basic stuff first.
Never be cruel, never be cowardly. And never ever eat pears! Remember – hate is always foolish…and love, is always wise.
Always try, to be nice and never fail to be kind. Oh, and….and you mustn’t tell anyone your name. No-one would understand it anyway. Except….
Except….children. Children can hear it. Sometimes – if their hearts are in the right place, and the stars are too. Children can hear your name.
But nobody else. Nobody else. Ever.
Laugh hard. Run fast. Be kind.
Doctor – I let you go.

Everyone talks about that last line being prophetic.  What about the second line?  Hmm...

author: @AwedecaiPlays
text:
The Doctor being British and/or white never--and I mean *NEVER* made me feel excluded, sad or self conscious just because I'm black. I never felt like Doctor Who was for any one person, sexuaIity, party or other. It was for everyone, because it told *good stories* we could all be deeply engaged in, that didn't appeal to skin deep sensibilities. Now, the 88C's actions are more discriminatory than ever with it's heavy-handed nagging and forced poIitics. But the biggest sin is the showrunners using poItical activism to tear down something that was truly amazing: Their own fans and their own work! I will never forgive the 88C for turning on us. Ever.

I *told* Doctor Who fans not to celebrate just because RTD was returning, because it's likely he wouldn't be the same man from 2005. And I was right.

Thank you for standing up for us nerds and non-nerds, Nerdrotic! I wasn't expecting to be teary-eyed at the end. That goodbye was edited masterfully.

replies.text:
​​@mascot4950 it's almost like people are more than just superficial characteristics.

The thing about representation is, the more you actively try to represent something in media, the further you get from actually representing that thing.

For example, I'm bisexual myself, it is a part of who I am, but it doesn't define me or the way I act or talk in the overwhelming majority of situations. The best representations I've seen of Bi characters in media, have all been the characters where it's something you can infer about them, not something that is explicitly stated and used as a crutch for the entire personality to be built on.

Now, there are definitely people out there whose whole personality is based around the thin veneer of identity politics, but it's easier for them to related to someone who represents a more understated version of the, than it is for someone more nuanced to relate to the over zealous representation.

replies.author: @rowanthursday5860
replies.text:
@johndoe-so2ef Actually- although I hated the Whittaker era at the time, and only now find it vaguely watchable in contrast to the Gatwa era, Jodie Whittaker's strengths and weaknesses were, I think, very similar to those of Peter Davison as a Doctor. Davison famously, if slightly facetiously, described his approach as "get the script and learn the words", and it marks a slight contrast. Whereas Jon Pertwee and Tom Baker, for instance, did marvellously well with a good script, they also delivered a mesmerising performance with a bad script when they got one, and, for Tom Baker in particular, the *script* was just a minor detail of very little relevance to how he played the Doctor. Davison and Whittaker approached things rather differently- in terms of seeing their job as being to perform the part written for them. So both could do well with a good script, but *needed* a decent script. That's why Davison left, after dealing with a middle season of mediocre scripts, only to regret it after his third and last season had much better stories, but Whittaker, unfortunately, was saddled with Chibnall, and so only got a handful of good scripts, not nearly enough to cement her Doctor or make her even remotely memorable.
It's hard to tell what Gatwa's approach was. He seems to have completely misjudged the character, for a start, unless it was misjudged for him. It's sad to see how RTD's previous writing skill has utterly deserted him now- and more than a bit inexplicable.

​@eltetters7194 Concern that other people "think they're better than us" is kinda just telling on yourself. Normal, healthy people aren't preoccupied with concerns like that. They certainly aren't so much on a hair trigger that the instigating event (Dr. Who coming back as a female actor) would be enough to trigger the rage that it did. This is basically just people looking for misandry under every rock and branch. The normal reaction for a guy who suspects this is to keep quiet initially, hope nothing of the sort was meant, and then wait and see if it bears out.  That was not what happened though. What happened was a loud, public, flaming, and intemperate rage that they would dare cast a woman in that position. 

And how *_did_* you expect the producers to react to something like that? Did you really think it would make them go back on their decision (even if the decision was misguided), knowing that doing so would validate all the nastiness expressed in the backlash? To do so would essentially be communicating, "Yes, it was an affront to put a female lead here. By doing so, we have contaminated something sacred by allowing an unclean woman into its presence." Unless they were willing to make a gross statement like that, you had left them no choice but to go looking for a new audience, which they attempted rather clumsily.

Even Bill--who was arguably touted as a token character by the BBC (gay, black, female) I thought was well done (her sexuality and skin color were, most of the time, not the most important thing about her) and I loved her father (grandfather?)/daughter relationship with the Doctor. (and hey, I loved the Doctor/Rose, but. The longer that went on the unhealthier it got. replies.author: @TheRealLeroy
replies.text:
I tip my hat to you. That was a genuinely thought out and carefully crafted response. To piggy back on you thoughts. I'm sure it wasn't easy trudging through all the beloved IPs that they have since strip mined-- leaving a vacant, rotting, hollow, husk of their once glorious selves-- one that didn't need gimmicks, member berries, or dangling keys to beckon all to come and find rest from a weary world full of pandering and cynics. Bidding you to come walk the halls of real virtue where true artisans and masters of their craft refined and revealed story after story, with heart and depth. Stories that the people yearned and clamored for, that had easily stood the test of time. Only to see them as they are now, demolished to ruins; mere rubble to sift through, to find pieces of what once worked or could work to make the fans come back. I despise what some have done to our once brilliant creatives and entertainers. 

I think the following dialogue from Serenity sums it up all to well...

    The Operative: I'm sorry. If your quarry goes to ground, leave no ground to go to. You should have taken my offer. Or did you think none of this was your fault?
    Mal: I don't murder children.
    The Operative: I do, if I have to.
    Mal: Why? Do you even know why they sent you?
    The Operative: It's not my place to ask. I believe in something greater than myself. A better world. A world without sin.
    Mal: So me and mine gotta lay down and die so you can live in your better world?
    The Operative: I'm not going to live there... There's no place for me there, any more than there is for you. Malcolm, I'm a monster. What I do is evil. I have no illusions about it, but it
    must be done.

I found Doctor Who as a young, lonely kid on PBS 13 in syndication and fell in love with those old stories. When the New Doctor Who Series arrived with Eccleston, I fell in love again as a teen still trying to find my place. Those stories, the Doctor's Stories, were a touchstone all the way through my teen years up through my time into the Army and my first deployment and beyond. 

Then they killed the show.

Like so many other franchises, I, we have lost these touchstones and it hurts. It really, really hurts.

text: That fucking tribute at the end, I can’t 😭 I haven’t been around for the same amount of time as many Dr Who fans, only born in 2007, but the show holds such a special place in my heart. Watching episodes from Eccleston and Tennant on the old, lagging family laptop, late nights watching Tennant and Smith on DVD with my brother, pretending to be the Doctor with a light-up replica of 11’s sonic screwdriver, borrowing out the books from the library for a week or so and watching Capaldi’s season during some of the darkest times of my childhood to keep going. Watching the old seasons now and feeling like I’ve stepped back through time and feeling like an excited child from an era long gone. They can ruin the future of Doctor Who, they can get it cancelled again, they can strip it of everything that made it unique, but they can never take away what we already have. Every Doctor from 1 to 12 is still there, perfectly preserved, ready to welcome us back with open arms and take us back to that land of science and fantasy and emotion and wonder and for a moment, it can feel like nothing’s ever changed at all.

author: @oranblack
text:
The fall of Doctor Who felt like watching a loved one slowly die in hospice. It started off subtle- the quality slowly dropping here and there, things feeling out of place. Then it all just… went wrong. All at once. From there it was a slippery slope. Every time things looked to be improving, they just got worse, until you reach the point of no return. And all you can do is wait for them to finally die.

I’ve taken no pleasure in watching this show wither away. It was a massive part of my childhood- I watched it with my mother and sister every week, owned the shirts, the merch, the toys, got my friends into it- everything. And now there’s nothing left but a dying husk on life support, waiting to have its plug finally pulled.

author: @judigbr9430
text:
As a Brit who grew up with Dr Who (both my parents were fans and they grew up with it, too), it breaks my heart to see what they’ve done to the show. 
I was so excited when it came back with Chris Ecclestone, and I loved David Tenant’s original run. To see what it’s become, now, is super upsetting. 
It was a show for all of us who felt alone. We didn’t need anything pushed down our throats to enjoy it. It had good stories and was fun to watch. As a Hitchhiker’s Guide fan, one of my favourite eras is when Douglas Adams was writing it.
Ngl, this video brought tears to my eyes at how much we’ve lost. When it was first cancelled, it wasn’t as bad as this. It had run its course for the time and it was right to end it. This time, it was m*rdered by the people who were supposed to be looking after it.
